manifest.description: Blueprint-compiled draft dispatch pack for math.prob.conditional-probability (Phase 1 front-end; DRAFT only)

assets.title: math.prob.conditional-probability â€” cognitive map
assets.content:
### Target Understanding
**Conditional probability** P(A|B) is the probability of event A given that event B has already
occurred, defined as P(A|B) = P(A âˆ© B) / P(B), valid when P(B) > 0.  Conditioning on B restricts
the sample space from Î© to B: we ignore all outcomes outside B and rescale so the remaining
probabilities sum to 1.  This is the foundation of Bayesian reasoning.

### Conceptual Layers (C â†’ P â†’ A)
| Layer | Content |
|-------|---------|
| **Concrete (C)** | Card-drawing without replacement; two-step experiment with updated counts |
| **Pictorial (P)** | Venn diagram with B shaded as the new "universe"; reduced sample space |
| **Abstract (A)** | Formula P(A|B) = P(Aâˆ©B)/P(B); asymmetry P(A|B) â‰  P(B|A) in general |

### Prerequisite Knowledge (from KG)
- **math.prob.probability-axioms** â€” Kolmogorov axioms; P(Aâˆ©B), P(AâˆªB), P(A^c); probability measure

### Key Facts
| Statement | Formula |
|-----------|---------|
| Definition | P(A\|B) = P(A âˆ© B) / P(B), P(B) > 0 |
| Multiplication rule | P(A âˆ© B) = P(A\|B) Ã— P(B) = P(B\|A) Ã— P(A) |
| P(A\|B) is itself a probability measure on B | P(Î©\|B) = 1; P(A\|B) â‰¥ 0; disjoint additivity holds within B |
| Asymmetry | P(A\|B) â‰  P(B\|A) in general |
| Undefined when | P(B) = 0 |

---

<!-- COMPONENT 2: MISCONCEPTION REGISTRY -->

assets.title: CONDITIONAL-IS-JOINT
assets.content: | MC-1 | CONDITIONAL-IS-JOINT | Writes P(A\|B) = P(A âˆ© B) without dividing by P(B) | Reads "probability of A and B" rather than "probability of A given B"; missing the rescaling step | **FOUNDATIONAL** |

assets.title: REVERSING-CONDITIONING
assets.content: | MC-2 | REVERSING-CONDITIONING | Writes P(A\|B) = P(B\|A) always; confuses which event is the condition | "Given" and "of" are treated as symmetric; no awareness that swapping condition changes the value | Secondary |

assets.title: ZERO-DENOMINATOR-IGNORED
assets.content: | MC-3 | ZERO-DENOMINATOR-IGNORED | Attempts to compute P(A\|B) without verifying P(B) > 0; divides by zero without flagging | No habit of checking the denominator's validity before applying the formula | Secondary |

assets.title: Terminal Mastery Gate (P91)
assets.content:
### TA-A04 Â· Terminal Mastery Gate (P91)

```
P91 = P77 â†’ P55 â†’ P76 â†’ P55 â†’ P75 â†’ P55 â†’ P74 â†’ P55 â†’ P78
```

**[P77 â€” MULTI-PROBLEM SET]**  (4 questions)

**Q1.** P(A âˆ© B) = 0.15, P(B) = 0.25.  Find P(A|B).

*(Target: 0.15/0.25 = 0.6.)*

**Q2.** In a group of 100 people, 60 exercise and 30 eat healthy.  Of the 30 healthy eaters,
20 also exercise.  Given a person exercises, what is the probability they eat healthy?

*(Target: P(healthy âˆ© exercise) = 20/100 = 0.2; P(exercise) = 60/100 = 0.6;
P(healthy|exercise) = 0.2/0.6 = 1/3 â‰ˆ 0.333.)*

**Q3.** P(A) = 0.5, P(B|A) = 0.4.  Find P(A âˆ© B) using the multiplication rule.

*(Target: P(A âˆ© B) = P(B|A) Ã— P(A) = 0.4 Ã— 0.5 = 0.2.)*

**Q4.** TRUE or FALSE: "P(A|B) is always equal to P(B|A)."  If false, give a counterexample.

*(Target: FALSE.  Counterexample from TA-A03: P(A|C)=1 â‰  P(C|A)=1/3, or any numerical example
where P(A) â‰  P(B).)*

**[P55 â€” SCORE]**  Tally Q1â€“Q4.

---

**[P76 â€” TRANSFER PROBE]**  (P76_MODE = Independence)

*Independence probe â€” self-contained.*

> A box contains 5 red chips and 3 blue chips.  You draw two chips **without replacement**.
>
> (a) What is P(second chip is red | first chip was red)?
> (b) What is P(second chip is red | first chip was blue)?
> (c) Are P(second red | first red) and P(second red | first blue) equal?
>     What does this tell you about the draws?
> (d) Use the multiplication rule to find P(both chips are red).

*Expected answers:*
*(a) After removing one red chip: 4 red, 3 blue remain (7 total). P = 4/7.*
*(b) After removing one blue chip: 5 red, 2 blue remain (7 total). P = 5/7.*
*(c) 4/7 â‰  5/7 â€” the draws are NOT independent. Knowing the first outcome changes the
    probability of the second.*
*(d) P(both red) = P(first red) Ã— P(second red | first red) = (5/8) Ã— (4/7) = 20/56 = 5/14.*

**[P55 â€” SCORE]**  Award 1 point for P76 if all four parts correct; 0 otherwise.

---

**[P75 â€” MASTERY ASSESSMENT]**

```
PASS_CRITERION: 5 out of 5 items (4 P77 + 1 P76)
THRESHOLD:      0.90  â†’  âŒˆ0.90 Ã— 5âŒ‰ = 5
```

**[P55 â€” SCORE]**  Combine P77 tally + P76 score â†’ total out of 5.

**[P74 â€” ROUTING DECISION]**

- **Score = 5/5 â†’ MASTERY ACHIEVED.** Proceed to P78.
- **Score â‰¤ 4/5 â†’ Identify which items failed:**
  - Q1 wrong â†’ MC-1 residual â†’ TB-R01.
  - Q2 wrong â†’ identify whether error is in constructing P(Aâˆ©B) (counting) or dividing â†’ TB-R01
    if formula error; re-explain table-to-probability conversion if counting error.
  - Q3 wrong â†’ multiplication rule confusion â†’ re-read the key facts table and retry.
  - Q4 wrong â†’ MC-2 residual â†’ TB-R02.
  - P76 wrong â†’ (a)/(b) wrong â†’ TB-R01 (updated counts); (c) wrong â†’ re-read asymmetry;
    (d) wrong â†’ multiplication rule from Q3 repair.
  - After repair, re-administer only the failed item.

**[P55 â€” SCORE]**  Record repair outcome.

**[P78 â€” COMPLETION]**

```
BLUEPRINT_ID:    math.prob.conditional-probability
MASTERY_REACHED: true
UNLOCKS:         math.prob.independence, math.prob.bayes-theorem, math.prob.total-probability
NEXT_CONCEPT:    math.prob.independence  (or math.prob.total-probability)
SESSION_CLOSE:   "You can now compute P(A|B) for any events with P(B)>0, apply the
                  multiplication rule, and recognise that P(A|B) â‰  P(B|A) in general.
                  These skills directly feed into Bayes' theorem and the law of total
                  probability."
```

---

<!-- COMPONENT 5: PROTOCOL B (REPAIR) -->

## Component 5: Protocol B â€” Repair Chains

---

### TB-R01 Â· Repair: CONDITIONAL-IS-JOINT (MC-1)

**Trigger:** Student omits division by P(B); writes P(A|B) = P(A âˆ© B).

**Step 1 â€” Counting analogy.**
> Return to the card example: B = "red" (26 cards), A âˆ© B = "red hearts" (13 cards).
> If you only wrote P(A âˆ© B) = 13/52 = 0.25, you haven't used the information that the card IS red.
> Knowing B happened means you're only in the 26-card world now.
> So you count: 13 out of 26 = 13/26 = 0.5, not 13/52.

**Step 2 â€” The formula's purpose.**
> P(A|B) = P(A âˆ© B) / P(B).
> Dividing by P(B) rescales all probabilities inside B to sum to 1.
> P(B|B) = P(B)/P(B) = 1 â€” "given B, B is certain." That makes sense.
> But P(A âˆ© B) alone â‰¤ P(B), so P(A âˆ© B) / P(B) â‰¤ 1. âœ“

**Step 3 â€” Numeric drill.**
> Try: P(A âˆ© B) = 0.3, P(B) = 0.6. P(A|B) = 0.3 / 0.6 = 0.5.

**Exit:** Return to TA-A02 P49 checkpoint.

---

### TB-R02 Â· Repair: REVERSING-CONDITIONING (MC-2)

**Trigger:** Student writes P(A|B) = P(B|A) or swaps numerator/denominator.

**Step 1 â€” Two calculations side by side.**
> Events: A = "rolls 5 or 6", P(A)=1/3.  B = "rolls even", P(B)=1/2.  Aâˆ©B = {6}, P(Aâˆ©B)=1/6.
> P(A|B) = P(Aâˆ©B)/P(B) = (1/6)/(1/2) = 1/3.  "Given even, the die shows 5 or 6" â†’ only 6 âœ“.
> P(B|A) = P(Aâˆ©B)/P(A) = (1/6)/(1/3) = 1/2.  "Given 5 or 6, is it even?" â†’ only 6 of {5,6} âœ“.

**Step 2 â€” Denominator identification rule.**
> The denominator is ALWAYS the probability of the **condition** (the event after the "|").
> P(A|B): condition = B, denominator = P(B).
> P(B|A): condition = A, denominator = P(A).
> P(Aâˆ©B) is always the numerator regardless of which direction.

**Exit:** Return to TA-A03 P49 checkpoint.

---

### TB-R03 Â· Repair: ZERO-DENOMINATOR-IGNORED (MC-3)

**Trigger:** Student computes P(A|B) without checking P(B) > 0.

**Step 1 â€” Why P(B) = 0 is a problem.**
> P(A|B) means "given that B occurred."  If P(B) = 0, event B never occurs.
> Asking "given that something impossible happened" has no meaningful answer.
> Mathematically, P(A|B) = P(Aâˆ©B)/P(B) = P(Aâˆ©B)/0 is undefined.

**Step 2 â€” Build the habit.**
> Before computing P(A|B):
> Step 0: Check P(B) > 0.  If yes, proceed.  If no, write "undefined."

**Exit:** Continue with awareness of the check in subsequent problems.

---

<!-- COMPONENT 6: P89 SPACED REPETITION -->

## Component 6: P89 Spaced Repetition

```
P89 SCHEDULE:
  Interval-1 (next session):
    Probe: "P(Aâˆ©B)=0.12, P(B)=0.3. Find P(A|B)."
    Target: 0.12/0.3 = 0.4. PASS if correct.

  Interval-2 (+3 days):
    Probe: "P(A)=0.4, P(B)=0.6, P(Aâˆ©B)=0.2. Find P(A|B) and P(B|A). Are they equal?"
    Target: P(A|B)=0.2/0.6=1/3; P(B|A)=0.2/0.4=0.5. Not equal. PASS if both correct and
            student correctly says they are unequal.

  Interval-3 (+1 week):
    Probe: "P(A)=0.7, P(B|A)=0.3. Use the multiplication rule to find P(Aâˆ©B)."
    Target: P(Aâˆ©B)=P(B|A)Ã—P(A)=0.3Ã—0.7=0.21. PASS if correct.

REACTIVATION_TRIGGER: Any probe fails â†’ requeue TA-A02 (formula) or TA-A03 (asymmetry) as appropriate.
```

---

<!-- COMPONENT 7: CROSS-BLUEPRINT DEPENDENCIES -->

## Component 7: Cross-Blueprint Dependencies

```
PREREQUISITES_CONSUMED:
  math.prob.probability-axioms:
    Used in:   TA-A01 (P(Aâˆ©B) notation from prerequisite; probability as a measure on events),
               TA-A02 (P(Aâˆ©B) used in the formula; axiom A2 ensures P(B|B)=1),
               TA-A04 Q3 (multiplication rule P(Aâˆ©B)=P(B|A)Ã—P(A)), P76 (draws without replacement).
    Assumed:   Student can compute P(Aâˆ©B), P(AâˆªB); knows P(A)âˆˆ[0,1]; knows probability axioms.

UNLOCKS_ENABLED:
  math.prob.independence:
    Dependency: Independence is defined as P(A|B) = P(A), i.e., conditioning on B does not
                change the probability of A. Requires the conditional probability formula.
  math.prob.bayes-theorem:
    Dependency: Bayes' theorem P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B) rearranges the multiplication rule;
                requires understanding of both P(A|B) and P(B|A).
  math.prob.total-probability:
    Dependency: Total probability P(B) = Î£P(B|Aáµ¢)P(Aáµ¢) uses P(B|Aáµ¢) conditional probabilities.

CROSS_LINKS_NOTED:
  (none)
```

---

<!-- COMPONENT 8: TEACHING NOTES -->

## Component 8: Teaching Notes

**1. bloom = apply: formula execution is the primary goal.**
Students must be able to extract P(Aâˆ©B) and P(B) from problem descriptions (tables, words, or
given values) and correctly divide.  The card-draw and marble examples in TA-A01 provide the
Concrete anchoring needed before the abstract formula.  The P07 worked pair in TA-A02 models
the formula execution twice, including a common wrong approach.

**2. MC-1 is driven by notation confusion, not conceptual confusion.**
Most students understand that conditioning changes the sample space (they get the card example).
The error is algebraic: they write P(Aâˆ©B) instead of P(Aâˆ©B)/P(B).  The P64 explanation
("dividing by P(B) rescales probabilities to sum to 1") plus the P07 worked pair usually
resolves MC-1 in one pass.

**3. MC-2 (reversing conditioning) is the highest-stakes error.**
Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A) is the core error in prosecutor's fallacy reasoning (used in
legal/medical contexts).  TA-A03 Contrast A demonstrates this with both a symmetric and
asymmetric case to prevent over-generalisation from the symmetric coincidence.

**4. P76 probe exercises the multiplication rule forward.**
Parts (a)â€“(b) test the basic formula with updated counts; (c) introduces the concept of
independence (not yet taught) as a consequence; (d) applies the multiplication rule.  This
sequencing previews math.prob.independence without teaching it â€” appropriate at bloom=apply.

**5. The TA-A03 zero-denominator contrast is brief by design.**
MC-3 is a procedural gap, not a conceptual one.  The repair (TB-R03) is a habit-building step,
not a deep conceptual intervention.  Don't over-invest time here; flag and move on.

---

assets.title: Scaffolding Protocol
assets.content:
```
Entry â†’ TA-A01 (P03 card-draw analogy: updated counts after conditioning + P11 + P49)
      â†’ TA-A02 (P41/P64 MC-1 gate: formula P(A|B)=P(Aâˆ©B)/P(B) + P07 worked pair + P49)
      â†’ TA-A03 (P06 contrast: P(A|B) vs P(B|A); zero-denominator check + P49)
      â†’ TA-A04 (P91 terminal mastery gate)

Repair (Protocol B):
  MC-1 â†’ TB-R01 (rescaling the reduced sample space)
  MC-2 â†’ TB-R02 (P(A|B) vs P(B|A) â€” two explicit calculations)
  MC-3 â†’ TB-R03 (undefined conditional; check P(B)>0 first)
```
